story:
THE LAST BROADCAST

A small community radio station in the Mojave Desert has been playing the same overnight show for forty years.

Every night at 2:47 AM, the host reads the same weather, the same dedications, the same ads for businesses that closed decades ago. Listeners who call in sometimes get through. The voice on the other end knows their name. Knows things they never said on air.

Three months ago, a sound engineer went to the transmitter site to fix a fault. He never came back. His truck was found running, door open, coffee still warm. The station kept broadcasting.

You are a freelance journalist chasing the story. The station manager has stopped returning calls. The only lead is a handwritten note left at the front desk: Ask about the 2:47 club.

Setting: Desert town at night—dusty streets, neon diner, the radio station lobby, a trailer park under a single streetlight, the transmitter shack on a hill, an abandoned drive-in. Eerie, quiet, Americana gone slightly wrong.

Core mystery: The overnight show is not a recording. The host is real—or was. He died in 1984 when the station tower was struck by lightning. His final broadcast never ended. Listeners who fall asleep to his voice sometimes wake up in a version of the town that never moved past that night. The 2:47 club are people who have been there for years and don't age. They are trying to find a way to let the host rest—and to go home.

NPCs with clues: the graveyard-shift diner waitress who has heard every show and never sleeps; the retired DJ who was there the night of the strike; a teenager who claims she has been to the other town; and the station manager, who has started to forget which version of the town he belongs to.

The truth: The broadcast is a loop of the host's last moments, sustained by the collective attention of everyone who ever listened. To end it, someone has to stay on the line at 2:47 and tell him it's okay to sign off—but whoever does that might take his place in the loop.

Dark ending: You sit in the studio at 2:47. The mic is live. You hear your own voice on the monitors, reading the weather. You don't remember speaking. Through the window, the desert is still. The tower lights blink. And somewhere, in another version of this night, the real you is still driving toward the station, coffee in hand, never quite arriving.
